text: Samuel died, and all Israel gathered to mourn him and bury him at his home in Ramah; David then went down into the wilderness near Maon.
A man in Maon who owned property at Carmel was very rich, with three thousand sheep and a thousand goats, and he was busy shearing his sheep at Carmel.
The man's name was Nabal, harsh and mean in his dealings; his wife Abigail was intelligent and lovely, though he came from a stubborn Calebite line.
When David heard in the wilderness that Nabal was shearing his sheep,
he sent ten young men, telling them to go up to Nabal at Carmel and greet him in his name.
They were to ask Nabal to treat his men kindly and give them whatever provisions he had on hand, since it was a festive day.
Nabal answered David's men by asking who this David even was, as if he were just another runaway servant these days.
He refused to hand his bread, water, and freshly slaughtered meat over to men who had simply shown up from who knows where.
Hearing the report, David told his men to strap on their swords, and about four hundred of them set out with him, leaving two hundred to guard the supplies.
One of the servants told Abigail how David's messengers had greeted Nabal kindly and been answered with insults instead.
Abigail quickly gathered two hundred loaves, two skins of wine, five dressed sheep, roasted grain, a hundred raisin cakes, and two hundred fig cakes, and loaded them onto donkeys.
As she rode down through a hidden ravine, David and his men were coming down from the other direction, and they met her there.
When Abigail saw David, she quickly got down from the donkey and bowed low before him with her face to the ground.
She told David that the LORD himself had kept him from bloodshed and from taking revenge with his own hand, and asked that his enemies fare like Nabal.
David blessed the LORD for sending Abigail to meet him that day.
He praised her good judgment and thanked her for keeping him from bloodguilt and from avenging himself with his own hand.
David accepted what she had brought, told her to go home in peace, and said he had listened to her and granted her request.
Back home, Abigail found Nabal feasting like a king, drunk and in high spirits, so she waited until morning to tell him anything.
In the morning, once Nabal had sobered up, his wife told him everything, and his heart failed within him; he turned as still as a stone.
About ten days later, the LORD struck Nabal, and he died.
When David heard Nabal had died, he blessed the LORD for settling the score over Nabal's insult and for keeping David himself from wrongdoing, then sent word asking Abigail to become his wife.
Abigail hurried to David with her five young women in attendance, riding on a donkey, and she became his wife.
Meanwhile Saul had already given his daughter Michal, David's wife, to Palti son of Laish, from Gallim.
